text:
It was already deep into the night by the time they said goodbye. Caleb pulled his brown leather coat tight against the cold and started for home. His thoughts chased one another: whether Will could really arrange for them to see the ship; that by now he really ought to have asked for Heloise’s hand; and then his friend’s larger question—whether any of it was enough for him.

His feet carried him down to the docks. In the taverns there, the night was only just beginning, but he never had the nerve to blend in with the sailors and listen to their drunken tales of the sea. Yet it might be that those who had truly been out there had a clearer view of what happened between the waves than they did from the Academy’s white marble buildings.

He sat on the edge of the pier and let his legs dangle. No stars showed behind the clouds; along the black horizon, sea and sky ran together. An unknown world lay out there, one he could only imagine and never reach.

From the inside pocket of his coat he drew two letters. One he had written to his father, reporting on his daily life. The other, much shorter, to his mother in her native tongue—a language whose spelling he did not know, if it had rules at all—so he used the conventions of the northern islands. It was getting harder to take up a pen, and he had less and less to say, so as usual he only assured her that he was well. Everything was fine. He chose to think that at least it kept him from forgetting the mother tongue he otherwise never used. But he did not set down what troubled his heart.

Perhaps he could have told Will tonight. The perfect chance was here, and still he did not. To say it aloud would be to admit he was not as content with his life as he claimed.

He drew a deep breath of the cold, salty air. Mother Ocean was boundless and eternal. She would wait for him, even if their meeting came on the day of his funeral.

He stood and slid the letters back into his coat pocket. He decided that before going home he would go back toward the Academy, where there was a post office open day and night for mad scientists like him and Lord Ebbing. He suspected he would not sleep more than a few hours that night either.
